personality_voice.book_baseline: In the books, Amos Diggory is defined by a specific and somewhat uncomfortable combination of traits: genuine warmth and sociability on the surface, but also a competitive, boastful pride in Cedric that tips into tactlessness and mild cruelty toward Harry. He is voluble, loud, and physically expressive ('roared genially', 'slapping his son on his back'). He speaks in long, enthusiastic bursts. He is also capable of professional assertiveness - he marches into the dark clearing at the campsite, squares his shoulders, and acts decisively. He is not a villain, but he is not simply a nice man either; his pride in Cedric leads him to needle Harry directly and publicly ('Bet you're not feeling quite as full of yourself now'), and he does so even when Cedric is visibly embarrassed. He backs down when challenged by authority (Crouch dresses him down and he reddens and mutters). His voice is characterized by enthusiasm, volume, and a slightly self-congratulatory tone.
personality_voice.film_portrayal: In the film, Amos is warm and sociable at the Portkey meeting, shakes Harry's hand, and says 'Great, great pleasure.' He is friendly and unremarkable. The boastful, competitive edge - the defining trait that makes him complex - is entirely absent. He does not needle Harry about Cedric's Quidditch victory. He does not make the pointed remark about Harry 'not feeling so full of himself.' His grief at Cedric's death ('That's my son! That's my boy!') is present and emotionally effective. His professional assertiveness at the campsite (marching into the dark, finding Winky) is cut entirely.
personality_voice.difference: The film removes the boastful, tactless, competitive dimension of Amos's personality. The book version uses Harry's fame as a foil for Cedric's achievements in a way that is socially awkward and mildly unkind. The film version is simply a pleasant, proud father. The grief scene is preserved and is the emotional peak of his film presence. His decisive professional behavior at the campsite is absent.
personality_voice.penalty_logic: The loss of the boastful/competitive trait is significant because it is the primary thing that distinguishes Amos from a generic proud father. Without it, his personality is recognizable in outline (proud of Cedric, warm, grieving) but the specific texture - the edge, the tactlessness, the way his pride in Cedric makes him slightly unpleasant - is gone. The grief scene partially compensates by showing emotional depth. Score sits in the 14-17 range: recognizable but with an important trait reduced.

narrative_role_agency.book_baseline: In the books, Amos serves several narrative functions: (1) he introduces the Diggory family and establishes Cedric as a rival/foil to Harry via the Quidditch story; (2) he acts as a competent Ministry official at the campsite - he is the one who physically investigates the dark, finds Winky, and drives the confrontation that leads to Winky's discovery; (3) he creates social friction by needling Harry at the Triwizard Tournament, which establishes the Diggory family's competitive relationship with Harry; (4) his grief at Cedric's death is a narrative function - it externalizes the human cost of Voldemort's return for the audience. He is active and initiating in the campsite scene.
narrative_role_agency.film_portrayal: In the film, Amos introduces the Portkey and greets Harry warmly. His campsite investigation and the Winky subplot are entirely cut (the Dark Mark/campsite sequence is present in the film but Amos's role in it is absent). His needling of Harry at the Tournament is cut. His grief at Cedric's death is preserved and is his most prominent moment. He is largely reactive and decorative except at the death scene.
narrative_role_agency.difference: The campsite investigation - where Amos is the most agentive character in the scene - is cut. His role as a source of social friction and competitive pressure on Harry is cut. What remains is his function as grieving father, which is preserved. Two of his three main narrative functions are removed.
narrative_role_agency.penalty_logic: The loss of the campsite agency and the Tournament friction reduces his narrative role substantially. He is no longer a character who acts and drives scenes; he is primarily a symbol of parental grief. This is a clear reduction in agency and narrative function, placing the score in the 10-13 range.

motivations_internal_conflict.book_baseline: Amos's motivations are relatively simple but specific: pride in Cedric, a desire to see Cedric recognized and celebrated (especially in contrast to Harry's fame), professional competence and duty, and love for his son. There is a mild internal tension between his genuine warmth and his competitive pride - he is not a bad person, but his need to elevate Cedric leads him to be unkind to Harry. His love for Cedric is the emotional core.
motivations_internal_conflict.film_portrayal: The film preserves the love for Cedric (grief scene) and the general warmth. The competitive pride and the tension it creates are absent. His motivations are simplified to 'loving father.'
motivations_internal_conflict.difference: The competitive dimension of his motivation - wanting Cedric recognized over Harry - is removed. What remains is the simpler, more sympathetic motivation of parental love.
motivations_internal_conflict.penalty_logic: The motivations are simplified but not wrong. The grief scene confirms the love for Cedric. The competitive pride is absent but the core motivation (love for his son) is intact. This is simplification rather than replacement, placing the score in the 8-10 range.

character_arc.book_baseline: Amos has a limited but real arc across Goblet of Fire: he begins as a proud, boastful father who uses Harry's fame to elevate Cedric, and ends as a devastated, broken parent. The arc is from competitive pride to grief - the very thing he boasted about (Cedric's superiority to Harry) is rendered meaningless by Cedric's death. This gives his arc a quiet tragic irony: his pride in Cedric's survival of the Quidditch match ('you didn't fall off, did you?') is answered by Cedric's death. The arc is not complex but it is meaningful.
character_arc.film_portrayal: The film preserves the endpoint of the arc (grief at Cedric's death) but removes the starting point (competitive pride, boasting). The arc therefore exists in compressed form - we see the grief but not the pride that gives it its specific tragic weight. The film does show him as a proud father at the Tournament (holding Cedric's hand up to the crowd), which partially substitutes for the book's boasting.
character_arc.difference: The arc's starting point is softened. The tragic irony - that his boasting about Cedric's superiority is answered by Cedric's death - is weakened because the boasting is absent. The grief remains.
character_arc.penalty_logic: The arc is compressed but the emotional endpoint is preserved. The loss of the starting point's specificity means the arc is less resonant but still recognizable. Score in the 11-13 range: compressed but still faithful in its essential shape.

key_relationships.book_baseline: Amos's key relationship is with Cedric: it is defined by intense, sometimes overbearing pride and love. He boasts about Cedric to Harry's face, slaps him on the back, calls him 'always modest, always the gentleman.' Cedric is visibly embarrassed by his father's behavior. The relationship has a specific dynamic: Amos's love is genuine but expressed in a way that puts pressure on Cedric and creates social awkwardness. His relationship with Arthur Weasley is collegial and warm. His relationship with Harry is defined by competitive friction.
key_relationships.film_portrayal: The film preserves the love between Amos and Cedric (the pre-maze 'good luck, my boy' exchange, the grief scene). The dynamic of Cedric being embarrassed by his father's overbearing pride is absent - in the film, Cedric is simply a confident, gracious young man and Amos is a warm father. The competitive friction with Harry is absent. The relationship with Arthur is briefly warm.
key_relationships.difference: The specific dynamic of Amos's overbearing pride and Cedric's embarrassment is lost. The relationship is simplified to straightforward parental love. The competitive dimension of the Amos-Harry relationship is absent.
key_relationships.penalty_logic: The emotional core of the Amos-Cedric relationship (love) is preserved, but the specific texture (overbearing pride, Cedric's embarrassment) is simplified. This is a meaningful change in dynamic but not a complete loss. Score in the 7-8 range.

complexity_nuance_lost_material.book_baseline: Amos's complexity comes from the combination of genuine warmth and competitive tactlessness. He is not a villain but he is not simply sympathetic either. His boasting at Harry's expense, his professional assertiveness at the campsite, his embarrassed retreat when Crouch dresses him down, and his grief all together create a character who is recognizably human in his flaws. The campsite scene in particular shows a different side: competent, brave, decisive.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.film_portrayal: The film reduces Amos to two notes: warm proud father and devastated grieving father. The tactless competitive edge, the professional competence at the campsite, and the embarrassed retreat before Crouch are all absent. The grief scene is well-executed and emotionally genuine.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.difference: The campsite material (professional competence, bravery, the Winky confrontation, the dressing-down by Crouch) is entirely lost. The competitive/tactless dimension is lost. What remains is the sympathetic dimension only.
complexity_nuance_lost_material.penalty_logic: The lost material is significant: it is what prevents Amos from being a one-dimensional grieving father. Without the campsite scene and the Tournament friction, the film Amos is simpler and more purely sympathetic than the book Amos. This is a noticeable simplification placing the score in the 8-10 range.

lost_or_transferred_material: Amos boasting about Cedric beating Harry at Quidditch to Harry's face at the Portkey meeting
Amos needling Harry at the Triwizard Tournament ('Bet you're not feeling quite as full of yourself now')
Amos marching into the dark clearing at the campsite to investigate the Dark Mark
Amos finding Winky unconscious and carrying her out of the trees
Amos accusing Winky/Crouch's household in the campsite confrontation
Amos being dressed down by Bartemius Crouch Sr. and reddening with embarrassment
Cedric being visibly embarrassed by his father's boasting
Amos's Floo Network appearance reporting Muggle disturbances
The tragic irony of Amos's boasting about Cedric's survival being answered by Cedric's death
score_caps_applied: Cap 3 (score above 75 if a core book function or act of agency is transferred or removed): Amos's campsite agency - the most active scene he has in the books - is entirely cut. Cap applied, preventing score above 75.
Cap 1 (score above 85 if films remove a major part of complexity or narrative function): Amos's competitive/tactless dimension and campsite function are removed. Cap reinforces ceiling well below 85.
comparative.key_observations: The film preserves Amos Diggory's emotional core - a loving, proud father whose grief at Cedric's death is genuine and affecting - but removes the specific traits that make him complex in the books: the competitive boasting at Harry's expense, the professional assertiveness and bravery at the campsite, and the embarrassed retreat before Crouch. The film Amos is more purely sympathetic and less textured. His grief scene is his most prominent moment in both versions, but in the books it lands against a backdrop of established pride and tactlessness that gives it additional weight. The campsite sequence, where Amos is at his most agentive and professionally competent, is entirely absent from the film. The result is a recognizable but simplified character - a grieving father rather than a proud, flawed, competent man whose love for his son was also his defining weakness.
